sound_type: loud raspy ka-arr -- single emphatic descending call approximately 1 second duration; fundamental frequency 800-2,000 Hz; lower-pitched than Common Tern keerr (2,000-4,000 Hz); carrying distance 300-500 meters across open estuary; continuous colony ambient from multiple calling pairs
ecosystem: Estuary -- river mouths, estuarine mudflats, large coastal lagoons, sandy shores adjacent to productive water; hover-and-plunge foraging for juvenile salmonids and forage fish; colonial nesting on adjacent islands or spits
season: Summer (breeding May-August; foraging April-September; a few winter along Oregon-California coast)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Large stable global population. Pacific Northwest colony management active due to predation on endangered salmonids. East Sand Island colony substantially reduced from 10,000 breeding pairs peak through USFWS hazing and habitat management.
milestone: Fifth Laridae entry (Laridae postscript after arc closure at 292). FIRST ESTUARY ECOSYSTEM entry in SPS engine. World's largest tern species. Caspian Tern closes the Laridae chapter before Alcidae begins at 294.

coordinates.description: East Sand Island, Columbia River mouth (Oregon/Washington border): formerly largest Caspian Tern colony in world; Puget Sound sites: Commencement Bay, Nisqually River delta, Padilla Bay. Migratory: winters California/Mexico/Central America coasts.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Single emphatic descending call 'ka-arr' or 'kaar'. Not colony screaming (292 Common Tern) but individual calls with high carrying power across open water.
acoustic_data.ka_arr_call: Loud, raspy, emphatic. Approximately 1 second duration. Descending rasp: begins higher frequency, drops in pitch across syllable. Fundamental 800-2,000 Hz.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 800-2,000 Hz fundamental. Substantially lower than Common Tern keerr (2,000-4,000 Hz). The frequency difference audibly represents the size difference: world's largest tern has the lowest-frequency tern call.
acoustic_data.carrying_distance: 300-500 meters in calm conditions. Exceptional for tern-sized bird. Low frequency + large syrinx = unusual projection across open water.
acoustic_data.e6_calibration: E=6: loud and projecting but single-call structure (not continuous rapid-fire alarm) and lower frequency produce driving rather than piercing quality. The Caspian Tern's ka-arr is powerful and direct; it is not the staccato urgency of E=7 tern colony screaming.

nodes.title: World's Largest Tern -- Hydroprogne caspia
nodes.description: The Caspian Tern is the largest tern species globally, with wingspan exceeding many gulls (50-57 inches). Monotypic genus Hydroprogne. Blood-red bill, solid black cap year-round, hover-and-plunge predation on large fish. The tern family's extreme in size, call power, and projection distance.

nodes.title: East Sand Island -- Columbia River Mouth Management
nodes.description: Columbia River mouth managed seabird colony. Formerly largest Caspian Tern colony in world (up to 10,000 breeding pairs). USFWS management to reduce predation on endangered Chinook and steelhead salmon smolts: one of PNW's most significant wildlife management conflicts. Colony partially relocated to inland sites.

nodes.title: Laridae Postscript -- Fifth Entry After Arc Closure
nodes.description: The Laridae arc closed formally at degree 292 (four entries: 289-292). The Caspian Tern at 293 is the fifth Laridae entry, arriving after the arc's formal closure as the family's most ecologically significant PNW representative. Closes the Laridae chapter before Alcidae opens at 294.

nodes.title: First Estuary Ecosystem Entry in SPS Engine
nodes.description: The Caspian Tern at degree 293 introduces estuary habitat to the SPS engine: river mouths, estuarine mudflats, the transition zone between river and ocean where Pacific Northwest productivity is highest and fish runs concentrate. The estuary ecosystem differs from coastal shelf (preceding Laridae) and rocky island (upcoming Alcidae).

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=6 = E=13. Both direct and undecorated: Sub Pop grunge/punk driving energy pairs with the largest tern's loud projecting ka-arr. Two entries defined by forward projection without decoration.

Previous Laridae (fifth in series). Common Tern keerr at 2,000-4,000 Hz; Caspian Tern ka-arr at 800-2,000 Hz. The world's largest tern is lower-pitched than the common medium tern: size and frequency inversely correlated.
FIRST ALCIDAE follows. Caspian Tern closes the Laridae chapter at 293; Tufted Puffin opens the Alcidae chapter at 294. The transition from coastal tern to rocky island alcid.
